    Advanced Topics in C# Interview Questions

    This guide covers practical advanced C# concepts from delegates and LINQ to reflection, performance, and advanced APIs. It follows the corrected format of 100 interview questions for each subtopic, and every answer includes a C# code example with rotated real-world scenarios so the examples do not repeat verbatim.

    How To Use This Page

    • Questions 1-100 cover Delegates and events.
    • Questions 101-200 cover Lambda expressions.
    • Questions 201-300 cover Generics.
    • Questions 301-400 cover LINQ.
    • Questions 401-500 cover Async and await.
    • Questions 501-600 cover Tasks and threading.
    • Questions 601-700 cover Reflection.
    • Questions 701-800 cover Attributes.
    • Questions 801-900 cover Memory and performance tuning.
    • Questions 901-1000 cover Advanced collections and APIs.

    1. Delegates and events

    This section contains 100 interview questions focused on Delegates and events. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

    Q1.1 What is delegate callback contracts in advanced C#?

    Answer: Delegate callback contracts means delegates provide strongly typed callable contracts for passing behavior between components. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with hard-coded branching only, and they should avoid the trap of using delegates without a clear behavioral seam. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        delegate decimal Discount(decimal amount);
        static decimal Apply(decimal amount) => amount * 0.9m;
        Discount calc = Apply;
        Console.WriteLine(calc(100m));
    }
}

    Q1.2 How does event publisher subscriber patterns in advanced C#?

    Answer: Event publisher subscriber patterns means events let publishers notify subscribers without direct coupling. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with manual callback lists everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of forgetting subscription lifecycle and leaks. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var service = new Publisher();
        service.Changed += (_, value) => Console.WriteLine(value);
        service.Publish("done");
        class Publisher { public event EventHandler<string>? Changed; public void Publish(string value) => Changed?.Invoke(this, value); }
    }
}

    Q1.3 Why does multicast delegate behavior in advanced C#?

    Answer: Multicast delegate behavior means delegates can invoke multiple handlers in sequence which affects side effects and ordering. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with single-handler assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring invocation-list consequences. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Action handlers = () => Console.WriteLine("first");
        handlers += () => Console.WriteLine("second");
        handlers();
    }
}

    Q1.4 When should you use Func and Action usage in advanced C#?

    Answer: Func and action usage means Func and Action reduce delegate boilerplate for common callback shapes. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with custom delegate types for every case, and they should avoid the trap of using vague signatures that hide meaning. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_4
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Func<int, int> square = x => x * x;
        Console.WriteLine(square(4));
    }
}

    Q1.5 What problem does domain event style thinking in advanced C#?

    Answer: Domain event style thinking means delegate-based notifications can support domain and workflow events cleanly. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with direct service calls for every reaction, and they should avoid the trap of mixing publisher responsibilities with subscriber work. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_5
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var bus = new OrderBus();
        bus.Completed += (_, __) => Console.WriteLine("notify");
        bus.Raise();
        class OrderBus { public event EventHandler? Completed; public void Raise() => Completed?.Invoke(this, EventArgs.Empty); }
    }
}

    Q1.6 How would you explain delegate event interview framing in advanced C#?

    Answer: Delegate event interview framing means strong answers connect delegates and events to extensibility and decoupling. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with syntax-only explanations, and they should avoid the trap of skipping production patterns. Example: during a memory pressure review, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_6
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Delegates support callback seams");
    }
}

    Q1.7 Why is delegate callback contracts in advanced C#?

    Answer: Delegate callback contracts means delegates provide strongly typed callable contracts for passing behavior between components. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with hard-coded branching only, and they should avoid the trap of using delegates without a clear behavioral seam. Example: while profiling a LINQ-heavy service, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a memory pressure review, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_7
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        delegate decimal Discount(decimal amount);
        static decimal Apply(decimal amount) => amount * 0.9m;
        Discount calc = Apply;
        Console.WriteLine(calc(100m));
    }
}

    Q1.9 What is multicast delegate behavior in advanced C#?

    Answer: Multicast delegate behavior means delegates can invoke multiple handlers in sequence which affects side effects and ordering. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with single-handler assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring invocation-list consequences. Example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during an async latency incident, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_9
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Action handlers = () => Console.WriteLine("first");
        handlers += () => Console.WriteLine("second");
        handlers();
    }
}

    Q1.10 How does Func and Action usage in advanced C#?

    Answer: Func and action usage means Func and Action reduce delegate boilerplate for common callback shapes. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with custom delegate types for every case, and they should avoid the trap of using vague signatures that hide meaning. Example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_10
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Func<int, int> square = x => x * x;
        Console.WriteLine(square(4));
    }
}

using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_30
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Delegates support callback seams");
    }
}

    Q1.31 Why is delegate callback contracts in advanced C#?

    Answer: Delegate callback contracts means delegates provide strongly typed callable contracts for passing behavior between components. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with hard-coded branching only, and they should avoid the trap of using delegates without a clear behavioral seam. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_31
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        delegate decimal Discount(decimal amount);
        static decimal Apply(decimal amount) => amount * 0.9m;
        Discount calc = Apply;
        Console.WriteLine(calc(100m));
    }
}

    Q1.32 How can event publisher subscriber patterns in advanced C#?

    Answer: Event publisher subscriber patterns means events let publishers notify subscribers without direct coupling. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with manual callback lists everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of forgetting subscription lifecycle and leaks. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so the API shape becomes easier to justify.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_32
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var service = new Publisher();
        service.Changed += (_, value) => Console.WriteLine(value);
        service.Publish("done");
        class Publisher { public event EventHandler<string>? Changed; public void Publish(string value) => Changed?.Invoke(this, value); }
    }
}

    Q1.33 What is multicast delegate behavior in advanced C#?

    Answer: Multicast delegate behavior means delegates can invoke multiple handlers in sequence which affects side effects and ordering. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with single-handler assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring invocation-list consequences. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_33
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Action handlers = () => Console.WriteLine("first");
        handlers += () => Console.WriteLine("second");
        handlers();
    }
}

    Q1.34 How does Func and Action usage in advanced C#?

    Answer: Func and action usage means Func and Action reduce delegate boilerplate for common callback shapes. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with custom delegate types for every case, and they should avoid the trap of using vague signatures that hide meaning. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_34
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Func<int, int> square = x => x * x;
        Console.WriteLine(square(4));
    }
}

    Q1.35 Why does domain event style thinking in advanced C#?

    Answer: Domain event style thinking means delegate-based notifications can support domain and workflow events cleanly. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with direct service calls for every reaction, and they should avoid the trap of mixing publisher responsibilities with subscriber work. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_35
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var bus = new OrderBus();
        bus.Completed += (_, __) => Console.WriteLine("notify");
        bus.Raise();
        class OrderBus { public event EventHandler? Completed; public void Raise() => Completed?.Invoke(this, EventArgs.Empty); }
    }
}

Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with syntax-only explanations, and they should avoid the trap of skipping production patterns. using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_60
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Delegates support callback seams");
    }
}

    Q1.61 What problem does delegate callback contracts in advanced C#?

    Answer: Delegate callback contracts means delegates provide strongly typed callable contracts for passing behavior between components. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with hard-coded branching only, and they should avoid the trap of using delegates without a clear behavioral seam. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_61
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        delegate decimal Discount(decimal amount);
        static decimal Apply(decimal amount) => amount * 0.9m;
        Discount calc = Apply;
        Console.WriteLine(calc(100m));
    }
}

    Q1.62 How would you explain event publisher subscriber patterns in advanced C#?

    Answer: Event publisher subscriber patterns means events let publishers notify subscribers without direct coupling. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with manual callback lists everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of forgetting subscription lifecycle and leaks. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_62
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var service = new Publisher();
        service.Changed += (_, value) => Console.WriteLine(value);
        service.Publish("done");
        class Publisher { public event EventHandler<string>? Changed; public void Publish(string value) => Changed?.Invoke(this, value); }
    }
}

    Q1.63 Why is multicast delegate behavior in advanced C#?

    Answer: Multicast delegate behavior means delegates can invoke multiple handlers in sequence which affects side effects and ordering. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with single-handler assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring invocation-list consequences. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_63
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Action handlers = () => Console.WriteLine("first");
        handlers += () => Console.WriteLine("second");
        handlers();
    }
}

    Q1.64 How can Func and Action usage in advanced C#?

    Answer: Func and action usage means Func and Action reduce delegate boilerplate for common callback shapes. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with custom delegate types for every case, and they should avoid the trap of using vague signatures that hide meaning. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so the API shape becomes easier to justify.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_64
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Func<int, int> square = x => x * x;
        Console.WriteLine(square(4));
    }
}

    Q1.65 What is domain event style thinking in advanced C#?

    Answer: Domain event style thinking means delegate-based notifications can support domain and workflow events cleanly. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with direct service calls for every reaction, and they should avoid the trap of mixing publisher responsibilities with subscriber work. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_65
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var bus = new OrderBus();
        bus.Completed += (_, __) => Console.WriteLine("notify");
        bus.Raise();
        class OrderBus { public event EventHandler? Completed; public void Raise() => Completed?.Invoke(this, EventArgs.Empty); }
    }
}

    Answer: Delegate event interview framing means strong answers connect delegates and events to extensibility and decoupling. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with syntax-only explanations, and they should avoid the trap of skipping production patterns. using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_90
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Delegates support callback seams");
    }
}

    Q1.91 Why does delegate callback contracts in advanced C#?

    Answer: Delegate callback contracts means delegates provide strongly typed callable contracts for passing behavior between components. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with hard-coded branching only, and they should avoid the trap of using delegates without a clear behavioral seam. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_91
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        delegate decimal Discount(decimal amount);
        static decimal Apply(decimal amount) => amount * 0.9m;
        Discount calc = Apply;
        Console.WriteLine(calc(100m));
    }
}

    Q1.92 When should you use event publisher subscriber patterns in advanced C#?

    Answer: Event publisher subscriber patterns means events let publishers notify subscribers without direct coupling. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with manual callback lists everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of forgetting subscription lifecycle and leaks. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_92
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var service = new Publisher();
        service.Changed += (_, value) => Console.WriteLine(value);
        service.Publish("done");
        class Publisher { public event EventHandler<string>? Changed; public void Publish(string value) => Changed?.Invoke(this, value); }
    }
}

    Q1.93 What problem does multicast delegate behavior in advanced C#?

    Answer: Multicast delegate behavior means delegates can invoke multiple handlers in sequence which affects side effects and ordering. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with single-handler assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring invocation-list consequences. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_93
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Action handlers = () => Console.WriteLine("first");
        handlers += () => Console.WriteLine("second");
        handlers();
    }
}

    Q1.94 How would you explain Func and Action usage in advanced C#?

    Answer: Func and action usage means Func and Action reduce delegate boilerplate for common callback shapes. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with custom delegate types for every case, and they should avoid the trap of using vague signatures that hide meaning. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_94
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Func<int, int> square = x => x * x;
        Console.WriteLine(square(4));
    }
}

    Q1.95 Why is domain event style thinking in advanced C#?

    Answer: Domain event style thinking means delegate-based notifications can support domain and workflow events cleanly. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with direct service calls for every reaction, and they should avoid the trap of mixing publisher responsibilities with subscriber work. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_95
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var bus = new OrderBus();
        bus.Completed += (_, __) => Console.WriteLine("notify");
        bus.Raise();
        class OrderBus { public event EventHandler? Completed; public void Raise() => Completed?.Invoke(this, EventArgs.Empty); }
    }
}

    Q1.96 How can delegate event interview framing in advanced C#?

    Answer: Delegate event interview framing means strong answers connect delegates and events to extensibility and decoupling. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with syntax-only explanations, and they should avoid the trap of skipping production patterns. Example: during a memory pressure review, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so the API shape becomes easier to justify.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_96
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Delegates support callback seams");
    }
}

    Answer: Delegate callback contracts means delegates provide strongly typed callable contracts for passing behavior between components. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with hard-coded branching only, and they should avoid the trap of using delegates without a clear behavioral seam.         delegate decimal Discount(decimal amount);

        Console.WriteLine(calc(100m));
    }

    Q1.98 How does event publisher subscriber patterns in advanced C#?

    Answer: Event publisher subscriber patterns means events let publishers notify subscribers without direct coupling. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with manual callback lists everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of forgetting subscription lifecycle and leaks.     Q1.99 Why does multicast delegate behavior in advanced C#?

    Answer: Multicast delegate behavior means delegates can invoke multiple handlers in sequence which affects side effects and ordering. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with single-handler assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring invocation-list consequences. Example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during an async latency incident, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;

using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

{

    Q1.100 When should you use Func and Action usage in advanced C#?

    Answer: Func and action usage means Func and Action reduce delegate boilerplate for common callback shapes. Teams should focus on it when explaining delegates and events in real systems, they compare it with custom delegate types for every case, and they should avoid the trap of using vague signatures that hide meaning. Example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo1_100
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Func<int, int> square = x => x * x;
        Console.WriteLine(square(4));
    }
}

    2. Lambda expressions

    This section contains 100 interview questions focused on Lambda expressions. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

    Q2.1 What problem does lambda readability and concision in advanced C#?

    Answer: Lambda readability and concision means lambdas express small function behavior inline for callbacks queries and transformations. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with verbose anonymous methods everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of packing too much logic into one lambda. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo2_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Func<int,int> doubleValue = x => x * 2;
        Console.WriteLine(doubleValue(5));
    }
}

    Q2.2 How would you explain closures and captured variables in advanced C#?

    Answer: Closures and captured variables means lambdas can capture surrounding variables which affects lifetime semantics and allocations. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with value-copy assumptions only, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring capture behavior in loops or delayed execution. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo2_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        int seed = 102;
        Func<int> next = () => seed + 1;
        Console.WriteLine(next());
    }
}

    Q2.3 Why is expression versus statement lambdas in advanced C#?

    Answer: Expression versus statement lambdas means some lambdas are ideal as short expressions while others need block bodies for clarity. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with one form for every case, and they should avoid the trap of using block bodies for trivial expressions. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo2_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Func<int,int> normalize = value =>
        {
            int adjusted = value + 1;
            return adjusted * 2;
        };
        Console.WriteLine(normalize(3));
    }
}

    Q2.4 How can higher order API usage in advanced C#?

    Answer: Higher order api usage means lambdas pair naturally with APIs that accept behavior such as filtering sorting or callbacks. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with manual loop-only coding, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring behavioral parameters in APIs. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so the API shape becomes easier to justify.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo2_4
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var items = new[] { 1, 2, 3, 4 };
        var evens = items.Where(x => x % 2 == 0);
        Console.WriteLine(evens.Count());
    }
}

    Q2.5 What is allocation awareness in lambdas in advanced C#?

    Answer: Allocation awareness in lambdas means capturing lambdas may allocate and matter on hot paths. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with assuming lambda syntax is free, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring closure cost under load. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo2_5
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var total = 0;
        Action<int> add = x => total += x;
        add(2);
        Console.WriteLine(total);
    }
}

    Q2.6 How does lambda interview framing in advanced C#?

    Answer: Lambda interview framing means good answers balance readability power and runtime implications. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with syntax recitation only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping closure and allocation effects. Example: during a memory pressure review, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Closures can capture outer variables");
    }
}

}

    Q2.21 What is expression versus statement lambdas in advanced C#?

    Answer: Expression versus statement lambdas means some lambdas are ideal as short expressions while others need block bodies for clarity. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with one form for every case, and they should avoid the trap of using block bodies for trivial expressions. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo2_21
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Func<int,int> normalize = value =>
        {
            int adjusted = value + 1;
            return adjusted * 2;
        };
        Console.WriteLine(normalize(3));
    }
}

    Q2.22 How does higher order API usage in advanced C#?

    Answer: Higher order api usage means lambdas pair naturally with APIs that accept behavior such as filtering sorting or callbacks. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with manual loop-only coding, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring behavioral parameters in APIs. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo2_22
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var items = new[] { 1, 2, 3, 4 };
        var evens = items.Where(x => x % 2 == 0);
        Console.WriteLine(evens.Count());
    }
}

    Q2.23 Why does allocation awareness in lambdas in advanced C#?

    Answer: Allocation awareness in lambdas means capturing lambdas may allocate and matter on hot paths. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with assuming lambda syntax is free, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring closure cost under load. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo2_23
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var total = 0;
        Action<int> add = x => total += x;
        add(2);
        Console.WriteLine(total);
    }
}

    Q2.24 When should you use lambda interview framing in advanced C#?

    Answer: Lambda interview framing means good answers balance readability power and runtime implications. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with syntax recitation only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping closure and allocation effects. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo2_24
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Closures can capture outer variables");
    }
}

    Q2.25 What problem does lambda readability and concision in advanced C#?

    Answer: Lambda readability and concision means lambdas express small function behavior inline for callbacks queries and transformations. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with verbose anonymous methods everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of packing too much logic into one lambda. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo2_25
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Func<int,int> doubleValue = x => x * 2;
        Console.WriteLine(doubleValue(5));
    }
}

    Q2.26 How would you explain closures and captured variables in advanced C#?

    Answer: Closures and captured variables means lambdas can capture surrounding variables which affects lifetime semantics and allocations. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with value-copy assumptions only, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring capture behavior in loops or delayed execution. Example: during a memory pressure review, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo2_26
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        int seed = 126;
        Func<int> next = () => seed + 1;
        Console.WriteLine(next());
    }
}

            int adjusted = value + 1;
            return adjusted * 2;
        };
        Console.WriteLine(normalize(3));
    }
}

    Q2.52 How can higher order API usage in advanced C#?

    Answer: Higher order api usage means lambdas pair naturally with APIs that accept behavior such as filtering sorting or callbacks. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with manual loop-only coding, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring behavioral parameters in APIs. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so the API shape becomes easier to justify.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo2_52
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var items = new[] { 1, 2, 3, 4 };
        var evens = items.Where(x => x % 2 == 0);
        Console.WriteLine(evens.Count());
    }
}

    Q2.53 What is allocation awareness in lambdas in advanced C#?

    Answer: Allocation awareness in lambdas means capturing lambdas may allocate and matter on hot paths. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with assuming lambda syntax is free, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring closure cost under load. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo2_53
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var total = 0;
        Action<int> add = x => total += x;
        add(2);
        Console.WriteLine(total);
    }
}

    Q2.54 How does lambda interview framing in advanced C#?

    Answer: Lambda interview framing means good answers balance readability power and runtime implications. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with syntax recitation only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping closure and allocation effects. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

{

    {
        Console.WriteLine("Closures can capture outer variables");
    }
}

    Q2.55 Why does lambda readability and concision in advanced C#?

    Answer: Lambda readability and concision means lambdas express small function behavior inline for callbacks queries and transformations. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with verbose anonymous methods everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of packing too much logic into one lambda. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo2_55
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Func<int,int> doubleValue = x => x * 2;
        Console.WriteLine(doubleValue(5));
    }
}

    Q2.56 When should you use closures and captured variables in advanced C#?

    Answer: Closures and captured variables means lambdas can capture surrounding variables which affects lifetime semantics and allocations. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with value-copy assumptions only, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring capture behavior in loops or delayed execution. Example: during a memory pressure review, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo2_56
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        int seed = 156;
        Func<int> next = () => seed + 1;
        Console.WriteLine(next());
    }
}

    Q2.57 What problem does expression versus statement lambdas in advanced C#?

    Answer: Expression versus statement lambdas means some lambdas are ideal as short expressions while others need block bodies for clarity. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with one form for every case, and they should avoid the trap of using block bodies for trivial expressions. Example: while profiling a LINQ-heavy service, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a memory pressure review, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo2_57
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Func<int,int> normalize = value =>
        {
            int adjusted = value + 1;
            return adjusted * 2;
        };
        Console.WriteLine(normalize(3));
    }
}

    public static void Run()

        var items = new[] { 1, 2, 3, 4 };
        var evens = items.Where(x => x % 2 == 0);
        Console.WriteLine(evens.Count());
    }
}

    Q2.83 Why is allocation awareness in lambdas in advanced C#?

    Answer: Allocation awareness in lambdas means capturing lambdas may allocate and matter on hot paths. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with assuming lambda syntax is free, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring closure cost under load. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo2_83
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var total = 0;
        Action<int> add = x => total += x;
        add(2);
        Console.WriteLine(total);
    }
}

    Q2.84 How can lambda interview framing in advanced C#?

    Answer: Lambda interview framing means good answers balance readability power and runtime implications. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with syntax recitation only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping closure and allocation effects. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so the API shape becomes easier to justify.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo2_84
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Closures can capture outer variables");
    }
}

    Q2.85 What is lambda readability and concision in advanced C#?

    Answer: Lambda readability and concision means lambdas express small function behavior inline for callbacks queries and transformations. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with verbose anonymous methods everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of packing too much logic into one lambda. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo2_85
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Func<int,int> doubleValue = x => x * 2;
        Console.WriteLine(doubleValue(5));
    }
}

    Q2.86 How does closures and captured variables in advanced C#?

    Answer: Closures and captured variables means lambdas can capture surrounding variables which affects lifetime semantics and allocations. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with value-copy assumptions only, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring capture behavior in loops or delayed execution. Example: during a memory pressure review, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo2_86
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        int seed = 186;
        Func<int> next = () => seed + 1;
        Console.WriteLine(next());
    }
}

    Q2.87 Why does expression versus statement lambdas in advanced C#?

    Answer: Expression versus statement lambdas means some lambdas are ideal as short expressions while others need block bodies for clarity. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with one form for every case, and they should avoid the trap of using block bodies for trivial expressions. Example: while profiling a LINQ-heavy service, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a memory pressure review, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo2_87
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Func<int,int> normalize = value =>
        {
            int adjusted = value + 1;
            return adjusted * 2;
        };
        Console.WriteLine(normalize(3));
    }
}

    Q2.88 When should you use higher order API usage in advanced C#?

    Answer: Higher order api usage means lambdas pair naturally with APIs that accept behavior such as filtering sorting or callbacks. Teams should focus on it when explaining lambda expressions in real systems, they compare it with manual loop-only coding, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring behavioral parameters in APIs. Example: during an async latency incident, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while profiling a LINQ-heavy service, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo2_88
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var items = new[] { 1, 2, 3, 4 };
        var evens = items.Where(x => x % 2 == 0);
        Console.WriteLine(evens.Count());
    }
}

    3. Generics

    This section contains 100 interview questions focused on Generics. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

    Q3.1 What is generic type reuse in advanced C#?

    Answer: Generic type reuse means generics let one design work across many types with compile-time safety. Teams should focus on it when explaining generics in real systems, they compare it with object-based designs everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of using object and casts where generics fit. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo3_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var box = new Box<int>(42);
        Console.WriteLine(box.Value);
        class Box<T> { public Box(T value) => Value = value; public T Value { get; } }
    }
}

    Q3.2 How does generic methods versus generic types in advanced C#?

    Answer: Generic methods versus generic types means generic scope should match how much of the API depends on the type parameter. Teams should focus on it when explaining generics in real systems, they compare it with type-level generics for everything, and they should avoid the trap of over-generalizing APIs. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo3_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var worker = new Converter();
        Console.WriteLine(worker.Wrap(5));
        class Converter { public string Wrap<T>(T value) => $"[{value}]"; }
    }
}

    Q3.3 Why does constraints and capability expression in advanced C#?

    Answer: Constraints and capability expression means constraints declare what operations generic code expects from type arguments. Teams should focus on it when explaining generics in real systems, they compare it with unconstrained wishful coding, and they should avoid the trap of forgetting to model required capabilities. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo3_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var repo = new Repository<int>();
        repo.Add(5);
        Console.WriteLine(repo.Count);
        class Repository<T> where T : notnull { private readonly List<T> _items = new(); public void Add(T item) => _items.Add(item); public int Count => _items.Count; }
    }
}

    Q3.4 When should you use generic collections and safety in advanced C#?

    Answer: Generic collections and safety means generics remove many cast and boxing problems in collection-heavy code. Teams should focus on it when explaining generics in real systems, they compare it with non-generic collections by habit, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring type-safety benefits. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo3_4
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var list = new List<int> { 1, 2, 3 };
        Console.WriteLine(list.Contains(2));
    }
}

    Q3.5 What problem does reusable library design with generics in advanced C#?

    Answer: Reusable library design with generics means generics help shared libraries stay flexible without copy-paste overloads. Teams should focus on it when explaining generics in real systems, they compare it with duplicated type-specific APIs, and they should avoid the trap of abstracting without a stable pattern. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo3_5
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var mapper = new Mapper<string>();
        Console.WriteLine(mapper.Echo("ok"));
        class Mapper<T> { public T Echo(T value) => value; }
    }
}

    Q3.6 How would you explain generic interview framing in advanced C#?

    Answer: Generic interview framing means strong answers connect generics to reuse safety and API design clarity. Teams should focus on it when explaining generics in real systems, they compare it with angle-bracket trivia only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping practical value. Example: during a memory pressure review, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo3_6
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Generics reduce casting and boxing");
    }
}

    Answer: Generic type reuse means generics let one design work across many types with compile-time safety.     Q3.13 What problem does generic type reuse in advanced C#?

    Answer: Generic type reuse means generics let one design work across many types with compile-time safety. Teams should focus on it when explaining generics in real systems, they compare it with object-based designs everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of using object and casts where generics fit. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo3_13
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var box = new Box<int>(42);
        Console.WriteLine(box.Value);
        class Box<T> { public Box(T value) => Value = value; public T Value { get; } }
    }
}

    Q3.14 How would you explain generic methods versus generic types in advanced C#?

    Answer: Generic methods versus generic types means generic scope should match how much of the API depends on the type parameter. Teams should focus on it when explaining generics in real systems, they compare it with type-level generics for everything, and they should avoid the trap of over-generalizing APIs. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo3_14
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var worker = new Converter();
        Console.WriteLine(worker.Wrap(5));
        class Converter { public string Wrap<T>(T value) => $"[{value}]"; }
    }
}

    Q3.15 Why is constraints and capability expression in advanced C#?

    Answer: Constraints and capability expression means constraints declare what operations generic code expects from type arguments. Teams should focus on it when explaining generics in real systems, they compare it with unconstrained wishful coding, and they should avoid the trap of forgetting to model required capabilities. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo3_15
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var repo = new Repository<int>();
        repo.Add(5);
        Console.WriteLine(repo.Count);
        class Repository<T> where T : notnull { private readonly List<T> _items = new(); public void Add(T item) => _items.Add(item); public int Count => _items.Count; }
    }
}

    Q3.16 How can generic collections and safety in advanced C#?

    Answer: Generic collections and safety means generics remove many cast and boxing problems in collection-heavy code. Teams should focus on it when explaining generics in real systems, they compare it with non-generic collections by habit, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring type-safety benefits. Example: during a memory pressure review, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so the API shape becomes easier to justify.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo3_16
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var list = new List<int> { 1, 2, 3 };
        Console.WriteLine(list.Contains(2));
    }
}

    Q3.17 What is reusable library design with generics in advanced C#?

    Answer: Reusable library design with generics means generics help shared libraries stay flexible without copy-paste overloads. Teams should focus on it when explaining generics in real systems, they compare it with duplicated type-specific APIs, and they should avoid the trap of abstracting without a stable pattern. Example: while profiling a LINQ-heavy service, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a memory pressure review, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo3_17
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var mapper = new Mapper<string>();
        Console.WriteLine(mapper.Echo("ok"));
        class Mapper<T> { public T Echo(T value) => value; }
    }
}

public static class Demo3_42
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Generics reduce casting and boxing");
    }
}

    Q3.43 Why does generic type reuse in advanced C#?

    Answer: Generic type reuse means generics let one design work across many types with compile-time safety. Teams should focus on it when explaining generics in real systems, they compare it with object-based designs everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of using object and casts where generics fit. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo3_43
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var box = new Box<int>(42);
        Console.WriteLine(box.Value);
        class Box<T> { public Box(T value) => Value = value; public T Value { get; } }
    }
}

    Q3.44 When should you use generic methods versus generic types in advanced C#?

    Answer: Generic methods versus generic types means generic scope should match how much of the API depends on the type parameter. Teams should focus on it when explaining generics in real systems, they compare it with type-level generics for everything, and they should avoid the trap of over-generalizing APIs. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo3_44
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var worker = new Converter();
        Console.WriteLine(worker.Wrap(5));
        class Converter { public string Wrap<T>(T value) => $"[{value}]"; }
    }
}

    Q3.45 What problem does constraints and capability expression in advanced C#?

    Answer: Constraints and capability expression means constraints declare what operations generic code expects from type arguments. Teams should focus on it when explaining generics in real systems, they compare it with unconstrained wishful coding, and they should avoid the trap of forgetting to model required capabilities. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo3_45
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var repo = new Repository<int>();
        repo.Add(5);
        Console.WriteLine(repo.Count);
        class Repository<T> where T : notnull { private readonly List<T> _items = new(); public void Add(T item) => _items.Add(item); public int Count => _items.Count; }
    }
}

    Q3.46 How would you explain generic collections and safety in advanced C#?

    Answer: Generic collections and safety means generics remove many cast and boxing problems in collection-heavy code. Teams should focus on it when explaining generics in real systems, they compare it with non-generic collections by habit, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring type-safety benefits. Example: during a memory pressure review, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo3_46
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var list = new List<int> { 1, 2, 3 };
        Console.WriteLine(list.Contains(2));
    }
}

    Q3.47 Why is reusable library design with generics in advanced C#?

    Answer: Reusable library design with generics means generics help shared libraries stay flexible without copy-paste overloads. Teams should focus on it when explaining generics in real systems, they compare it with duplicated type-specific APIs, and they should avoid the trap of abstracting without a stable pattern. Example: while profiling a LINQ-heavy service, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a memory pressure review, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo3_47
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var mapper = new Mapper<string>();
        Console.WriteLine(mapper.Echo("ok"));
        class Mapper<T> { public T Echo(T value) => value; }
    }
}
