    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_46
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        using var cts = new CancellationTokenSource();
        cts.Cancel();
        Console.WriteLine(cts.IsCancellationRequested);
    }
}

    Q6.47 Why does CPU-bound versus waiting-bound execution in advanced C#?

    Answer: Cpu-bound versus waiting-bound execution means threading choices depend on whether work consumes CPU or mostly waits. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with one execution strategy for all cases, and they should avoid the trap of using threads to hide waiting design issues. Example: while profiling a LINQ-heavy service, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a memory pressure review, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_47
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Task.Factory.StartNew(() => Thread.Sleep(10), TaskCreationOptions.LongRunning).Wait();
        Console.WriteLine("done");
    }
}

    Q6.48 When should you use task threading interview framing in advanced C#?

    Answer: Task threading interview framing means strong answers tie tasks and threads to scheduling blocking and scalability. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with API memorization only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping workload context. Example: during an async latency incident, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while profiling a LINQ-heavy service, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_48
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine(Thread.CurrentThread.ManagedThreadId);
    }
}

    Q6.49 What problem does Task versus Thread choice in advanced C#?

    Answer: Task versus thread choice means Task is usually the higher-level work abstraction while Thread is a lower-level execution primitive. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with treating them as interchangeable always, and they should avoid the trap of using raw threads without a specific reason. Example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during an async latency incident, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_49
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var thread = new Thread(() => Console.WriteLine(Environment.CurrentManagedThreadId));
        thread.Start();
        thread.Join();
    }
}

    Q6.50 How would you explain ThreadPool behavior in advanced C#?

    Answer: Threadpool behavior means the ThreadPool reuses workers and affects scalability under blocking and queued work. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with new threads for every task, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring pool starvation and blocking pressure. Example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_50
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Task task = Task.Run(() => Console.WriteLine("pool work"));
        task.Wait();
    }
}

    Q6.51 Why is coordination with WhenAll and continuations in advanced C#?

    Answer: Coordination with whenall and continuations means task-based code often needs structured coordination rather than one-off launches. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with fire-and-forget by default, and they should avoid the trap of starting concurrent work without join strategy. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_51
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var all = Task.WhenAll(Task.FromResult(1), Task.FromResult(2));
        Console.WriteLine(string.Join(",", all.Result));
    }
}

    Q6.52 How can cancellation token design in advanced C#?

    Answer: Cancellation token design means cooperative cancellation allows workloads to stop predictably. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with abrupt stop assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring cancellation in long-running work. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so the API shape becomes easier to justify.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_52
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        using var cts = new CancellationTokenSource();
        cts.Cancel();
        Console.WriteLine(cts.IsCancellationRequested);
    }
}

    Q6.77 What is CPU-bound versus waiting-bound execution in advanced C#?

    Answer: Cpu-bound versus waiting-bound execution means threading choices depend on whether work consumes CPU or mostly waits. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with one execution strategy for all cases, and they should avoid the trap of using threads to hide waiting design issues. Example: while profiling a LINQ-heavy service, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a memory pressure review, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_77
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Task.Factory.StartNew(() => Thread.Sleep(10), TaskCreationOptions.LongRunning).Wait();
        Console.WriteLine("done");
    }
}

    Q6.78 How does task threading interview framing in advanced C#?

    Answer: Task threading interview framing means strong answers tie tasks and threads to scheduling blocking and scalability. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with API memorization only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping workload context. Example: during an async latency incident, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while profiling a LINQ-heavy service, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_78
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine(Thread.CurrentThread.ManagedThreadId);
    }
}

    Q6.79 Why does Task versus Thread choice in advanced C#?

    Answer: Task versus thread choice means Task is usually the higher-level work abstraction while Thread is a lower-level execution primitive. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with treating them as interchangeable always, and they should avoid the trap of using raw threads without a specific reason. Example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during an async latency incident, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_79
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var thread = new Thread(() => Console.WriteLine(Environment.CurrentManagedThreadId));
        thread.Start();
        thread.Join();
    }
}

    Q6.80 When should you use ThreadPool behavior in advanced C#?

    Answer: Threadpool behavior means the ThreadPool reuses workers and affects scalability under blocking and queued work. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with new threads for every task, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring pool starvation and blocking pressure. Example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_80
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Task task = Task.Run(() => Console.WriteLine("pool work"));
        task.Wait();
    }
}

    Q6.81 What problem does coordination with WhenAll and continuations in advanced C#?

    Answer: Coordination with whenall and continuations means task-based code often needs structured coordination rather than one-off launches. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with fire-and-forget by default, and they should avoid the trap of starting concurrent work without join strategy. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_81
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var all = Task.WhenAll(Task.FromResult(1), Task.FromResult(2));
        Console.WriteLine(string.Join(",", all.Result));
    }
}

    Q6.82 How would you explain cancellation token design in advanced C#?

    Answer: Cancellation token design means cooperative cancellation allows workloads to stop predictably. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with abrupt stop assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring cancellation in long-running work. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo6_82
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        using var cts = new CancellationTokenSource();
        cts.Cancel();
        Console.WriteLine(cts.IsCancellationRequested);
    }
}

    Answer: Cpu-bound versus waiting-bound execution means threading choices depend on whether work consumes CPU or mostly waits. Teams should focus on it when explaining tasks and threading in real systems, they compare it with one execution strategy for all cases, and they should avoid the trap of using threads to hide waiting design issues.     7. Reflection

    This section contains 100 interview questions focused on Reflection. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

    Q7.1 What is assembly and type inspection in advanced C#?

    Answer: Assembly and type inspection means reflection can inspect assemblies types and members at runtime for metadata-driven behavior. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with hard-coded type references only, and they should avoid the trap of scanning everything without limits. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var assembly = typeof(string).Assembly;
        var type = assembly.GetType("System.String");
        Console.WriteLine(type?.FullName);
    }
}

    Q7.2 How does member invocation and dynamic access in advanced C#?

    Answer: Member invocation and dynamic access means reflection can invoke methods and inspect properties dynamically when needed. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with compile-time-only expectations, and they should avoid the trap of invoking members blindly without validation. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var members = typeof(DateTime).GetMembers();
        Console.WriteLine(members.Length > 0);
    }
}

    Q7.3 Why does runtime activation in advanced C#?

    Answer: Runtime activation means Activator and related APIs can instantiate types when exact types are discovered dynamically. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with manual construction lists only, and they should avoid the trap of hiding construction rules behind uncontrolled activation. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var instance = Activator.CreateInstance(typeof(List<int>));
        Console.WriteLine(instance?.GetType().Name);
    }
}

    Q7.4 When should you use reflection performance trade-offs in advanced C#?

    Answer: Reflection performance trade-offs means reflection is flexible but can be slower and allocate more than direct code paths. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with reflection is free enough everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of putting reflection on hot paths without caching. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_4
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var property = typeof(Uri).GetProperty("Host");
        Console.WriteLine(property?.Name);
    }
}

    Q7.5 What problem does framework and plugin use cases in advanced C#?

    Answer: Framework and plugin use cases means reflection powers many extensibility and framework discovery patterns. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with magic happens somehow explanations, and they should avoid the trap of using reflection without understanding cost boundaries. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_5
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var types = typeof(string).Assembly.GetTypes().Where(t => t.Name.Contains("String")).Take(2);
        Console.WriteLine(types.Count());
    }
}

    Q7.6 How would you explain reflection interview framing in advanced C#?

    Answer: Reflection interview framing means good answers connect reflection to frameworks plugins diagnostics and performance. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with member-name trivia only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping real architecture use. Example: during a memory pressure review, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_6
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(Guid).AssemblyQualifiedName is not null);
    }
}

    Q7.7 Why is assembly and type inspection in advanced C#?

    Answer: Assembly and type inspection means reflection can inspect assemblies types and members at runtime for metadata-driven behavior. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with hard-coded type references only, and they should avoid the trap of scanning everything without limits. Example: while profiling a LINQ-heavy service, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a memory pressure review, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_7
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var assembly = typeof(string).Assembly;
        var type = assembly.GetType("System.String");
        Console.WriteLine(type?.FullName);
    }
}

    Q7.8 How can member invocation and dynamic access in advanced C#?

    Answer: Member invocation and dynamic access means reflection can invoke methods and inspect properties dynamically when needed. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with compile-time-only expectations, and they should avoid the trap of invoking members blindly without validation. Example: during an async latency incident, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain. Another example: while profiling a LINQ-heavy service, so the API shape becomes easier to justify.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_8
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var members = typeof(DateTime).GetMembers();
        Console.WriteLine(members.Length > 0);
    }
}

    Q7.9 What is runtime activation in advanced C#?

    Answer: Runtime activation means Activator and related APIs can instantiate types when exact types are discovered dynamically. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with manual construction lists only, and they should avoid the trap of hiding construction rules behind uncontrolled activation. Example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during an async latency incident, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_9
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var instance = Activator.CreateInstance(typeof(List<int>));
        Console.WriteLine(instance?.GetType().Name);
    }
}

    Q7.10 How does reflection performance trade-offs in advanced C#?

    Answer: Reflection performance trade-offs means reflection is flexible but can be slower and allocate more than direct code paths. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with reflection is free enough everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of putting reflection on hot paths without caching. Example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_10
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var property = typeof(Uri).GetProperty("Host");
        Console.WriteLine(property?.Name);
    }
}

    Q7.11 Why does framework and plugin use cases in advanced C#?

    Answer: Framework and plugin use cases means reflection powers many extensibility and framework discovery patterns. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with magic happens somehow explanations, and they should avoid the trap of using reflection without understanding cost boundaries. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_11
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var types = typeof(string).Assembly.GetTypes().Where(t => t.Name.Contains("String")).Take(2);
        Console.WriteLine(types.Count());
    }
}

using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_39
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var instance = Activator.CreateInstance(typeof(List<int>));
        Console.WriteLine(instance?.GetType().Name);
    }
}

    Q7.40 How can reflection performance trade-offs in advanced C#?

    Answer: Reflection performance trade-offs means reflection is flexible but can be slower and allocate more than direct code paths. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with reflection is free enough everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of putting reflection on hot paths without caching. Example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain. Another example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so the API shape becomes easier to justify.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_40
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var property = typeof(Uri).GetProperty("Host");
        Console.WriteLine(property?.Name);
    }
}

    Q7.41 What is framework and plugin use cases in advanced C#?

    Answer: Framework and plugin use cases means reflection powers many extensibility and framework discovery patterns. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with magic happens somehow explanations, and they should avoid the trap of using reflection without understanding cost boundaries. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_41
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var types = typeof(string).Assembly.GetTypes().Where(t => t.Name.Contains("String")).Take(2);
        Console.WriteLine(types.Count());
    }
}

    Answer: Reflection interview framing means good answers connect reflection to frameworks plugins diagnostics and performance. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with member-name trivia only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping real architecture use. {

    {

}

    Q7.43 Why does assembly and type inspection in advanced C#?

    Answer: Assembly and type inspection means reflection can inspect assemblies types and members at runtime for metadata-driven behavior. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with hard-coded type references only, and they should avoid the trap of scanning everything without limits. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_43
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var assembly = typeof(string).Assembly;
        var type = assembly.GetType("System.String");
        Console.WriteLine(type?.FullName);
    }
}

    Q7.44 When should you use member invocation and dynamic access in advanced C#?

    Answer: Member invocation and dynamic access means reflection can invoke methods and inspect properties dynamically when needed. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with compile-time-only expectations, and they should avoid the trap of invoking members blindly without validation. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_44
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var members = typeof(DateTime).GetMembers();
        Console.WriteLine(members.Length > 0);
    }
}

    Q7.45 What problem does runtime activation in advanced C#?

    Answer: Runtime activation means Activator and related APIs can instantiate types when exact types are discovered dynamically. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with manual construction lists only, and they should avoid the trap of hiding construction rules behind uncontrolled activation. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;

using System.Threading;

        Console.WriteLine(instance?.GetType().Name);
    }
}

using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_71
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var types = typeof(string).Assembly.GetTypes().Where(t => t.Name.Contains("String")).Take(2);
        Console.WriteLine(types.Count());
    }
}

    Q7.73 What is assembly and type inspection in advanced C#?

    Answer: Assembly and type inspection means reflection can inspect assemblies types and members at runtime for metadata-driven behavior. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with hard-coded type references only, and they should avoid the trap of scanning everything without limits. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;

    Q7.74 How does member invocation and dynamic access in advanced C#?

    Answer: Member invocation and dynamic access means reflection can invoke methods and inspect properties dynamically when needed. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with compile-time-only expectations, and they should avoid the trap of invoking members blindly without validation. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

{

    {
        var members = typeof(DateTime).GetMembers();
        Console.WriteLine(members.Length > 0);
    }
}

    Q7.75 Why does runtime activation in advanced C#?

    Answer: Runtime activation means Activator and related APIs can instantiate types when exact types are discovered dynamically. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with manual construction lists only, and they should avoid the trap of hiding construction rules behind uncontrolled activation. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo7_75
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var instance = Activator.CreateInstance(typeof(List<int>));
        Console.WriteLine(instance?.GetType().Name);
    }
}

    Q7.76 When should you use reflection performance trade-offs in advanced C#?

    Answer: Reflection performance trade-offs means reflection is flexible but can be slower and allocate more than direct code paths. Teams should focus on it when explaining reflection in real systems, they compare it with reflection is free enough everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of putting reflection on hot paths without caching. Example: during a memory pressure review, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

        Console.WriteLine(property?.Name);
    }
}

    8. Attributes

    This section contains 100 interview questions focused on Attributes. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

    Q8.1 What problem does attribute metadata purpose in advanced C#?

    Answer: Attribute metadata purpose means attributes attach declarative metadata to code elements for later inspection. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with comments and naming conventions only, and they should avoid the trap of using attributes with no consumer. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [Demo("audit")]
        class AuditJob { }
        var attr = typeof(AuditJob).GetCustomAttributes(typeof(DemoAttribute), false).FirstOrDefault();
        Console.WriteLine(attr is not null);
        class DemoAttribute : Attribute { public DemoAttribute(string name) => Name = name; public string Name { get; } }
    }
}

    Q8.2 How would you explain attribute discovery via reflection in advanced C#?

    Answer: Attribute discovery via reflection means attributes matter when runtime code reads them to drive behavior validation or mapping. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with declaration-only thinking, and they should avoid the trap of adding attributes with no processing pipeline. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [RequiredField]
        class CustomerDto { public string Name { get; set; } = string.Empty; }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(CustomerDto).GetProperties().Length > 0);
        class RequiredFieldAttribute : Attribute { }
    }
}

    Q8.3 Why is attribute design and scope in advanced C#?

    Answer: Attribute design and scope means attribute types should express focused metadata clearly rather than become mini configuration objects. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with overloaded metadata bags, and they should avoid the trap of pushing too much behavior into attributes. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [RouteHint("orders")]
        class OrdersEndpoint { }
        var route = typeof(OrdersEndpoint).GetCustomAttributes(false).FirstOrDefault();
        Console.WriteLine(route?.GetType().Name);
        class RouteHintAttribute : Attribute { public RouteHintAttribute(string path) => Path = path; public string Path { get; } }
    }
}

    Answer: Framework attribute patterns means routing serialization validation and tooling often rely on attributes to express intent. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with framework magic assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring how metadata is consumed. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain. using System.Collections.Generic;

{

        Console.WriteLine(typeof(OldFlow).GetCustomAttributes(false).Length);
    }
}

    Q8.5 What is attributes versus business logic in advanced C#?

    Answer: Attributes versus business logic means attributes describe metadata while behavior usually belongs in code paths or services. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with full logic inside attributes, and they should avoid the trap of blurring declaration and execution boundaries. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_5
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [MapTo("invoice_total")]
        class InvoiceRow { public decimal Total { get; set; } }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(InvoiceRow).Name);
        class MapToAttribute : Attribute { public MapToAttribute(string field) => Field = field; public string Field { get; } }
    }
}

    Q8.6 How does attribute interview framing in advanced C#?

    Answer: Attribute interview framing means strong answers explain both declaration and consumption sides. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with square-bracket syntax only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping runtime reading and design trade-offs. Example: during a memory pressure review, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_6
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Attributes are useful only when code reads them");
    }
}

    Q8.7 Why does attribute metadata purpose in advanced C#?

    Answer: Attribute metadata purpose means attributes attach declarative metadata to code elements for later inspection. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with comments and naming conventions only, and they should avoid the trap of using attributes with no consumer. Example: while profiling a LINQ-heavy service, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a memory pressure review, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

{

    {
        [Demo("audit")]
        class AuditJob { }
        var attr = typeof(AuditJob).GetCustomAttributes(typeof(DemoAttribute), false).FirstOrDefault();
        Console.WriteLine(attr is not null);
        class DemoAttribute : Attribute { public DemoAttribute(string name) => Name = name; public string Name { get; } }
    }
}

using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_31
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [Demo("audit")]
        class AuditJob { }
        var attr = typeof(AuditJob).GetCustomAttributes(typeof(DemoAttribute), false).FirstOrDefault();
        Console.WriteLine(attr is not null);
        class DemoAttribute : Attribute { public DemoAttribute(string name) => Name = name; public string Name { get; } }
    }
}

    Q8.32 When should you use attribute discovery via reflection in advanced C#?

    Answer: Attribute discovery via reflection means attributes matter when runtime code reads them to drive behavior validation or mapping. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with declaration-only thinking, and they should avoid the trap of adding attributes with no processing pipeline. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_32
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [RequiredField]
        class CustomerDto { public string Name { get; set; } = string.Empty; }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(CustomerDto).GetProperties().Length > 0);
        class RequiredFieldAttribute : Attribute { }
    }
}

    Q8.33 What problem does attribute design and scope in advanced C#?

    Answer: Attribute design and scope means attribute types should express focused metadata clearly rather than become mini configuration objects. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with overloaded metadata bags, and they should avoid the trap of pushing too much behavior into attributes. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_33
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [RouteHint("orders")]
        class OrdersEndpoint { }
        var route = typeof(OrdersEndpoint).GetCustomAttributes(false).FirstOrDefault();
        Console.WriteLine(route?.GetType().Name);
        class RouteHintAttribute : Attribute { public RouteHintAttribute(string path) => Path = path; public string Path { get; } }
    }
}

    Q8.34 How would you explain framework attribute patterns in advanced C#?

    Answer: Framework attribute patterns means routing serialization validation and tooling often rely on attributes to express intent. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with framework magic assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring how metadata is consumed. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_34
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [Obsolete("Use NewFlow")]
        class OldFlow { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(OldFlow).GetCustomAttributes(false).Length);
    }
}

    Q8.35 Why is attributes versus business logic in advanced C#?

    Answer: Attributes versus business logic means attributes describe metadata while behavior usually belongs in code paths or services. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with full logic inside attributes, and they should avoid the trap of blurring declaration and execution boundaries. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_35
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [MapTo("invoice_total")]
        class InvoiceRow { public decimal Total { get; set; } }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(InvoiceRow).Name);
        class MapToAttribute : Attribute { public MapToAttribute(string field) => Field = field; public string Field { get; } }
    }
}

    Q8.36 How can attribute interview framing in advanced C#?

    Answer: Attribute interview framing means strong answers explain both declaration and consumption sides. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with square-bracket syntax only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping runtime reading and design trade-offs. Example: during a memory pressure review, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so the API shape becomes easier to justify.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_36
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Attributes are useful only when code reads them");
    }
}

    Q8.37 What is attribute metadata purpose in advanced C#?

    Answer: Attribute metadata purpose means attributes attach declarative metadata to code elements for later inspection. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with comments and naming conventions only, and they should avoid the trap of using attributes with no consumer. Example: while profiling a LINQ-heavy service, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a memory pressure review, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with square-bracket syntax only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping runtime reading and design trade-offs. Example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain. Another example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so the API shape becomes easier to justify.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_60
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Attributes are useful only when code reads them");
    }
}

    Q8.61 What is attribute metadata purpose in advanced C#?

    Answer: Attribute metadata purpose means attributes attach declarative metadata to code elements for later inspection. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with comments and naming conventions only, and they should avoid the trap of using attributes with no consumer. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_61
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [Demo("audit")]
        class AuditJob { }
        var attr = typeof(AuditJob).GetCustomAttributes(typeof(DemoAttribute), false).FirstOrDefault();
        Console.WriteLine(attr is not null);
        class DemoAttribute : Attribute { public DemoAttribute(string name) => Name = name; public string Name { get; } }
    }
}

    Q8.62 How does attribute discovery via reflection in advanced C#?

    Answer: Attribute discovery via reflection means attributes matter when runtime code reads them to drive behavior validation or mapping. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with declaration-only thinking, and they should avoid the trap of adding attributes with no processing pipeline. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_62
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [RequiredField]
        class CustomerDto { public string Name { get; set; } = string.Empty; }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(CustomerDto).GetProperties().Length > 0);
        class RequiredFieldAttribute : Attribute { }
    }
}

    Q8.63 Why does attribute design and scope in advanced C#?

    Answer: Attribute design and scope means attribute types should express focused metadata clearly rather than become mini configuration objects. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with overloaded metadata bags, and they should avoid the trap of pushing too much behavior into attributes. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_63
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [RouteHint("orders")]
        class OrdersEndpoint { }
        var route = typeof(OrdersEndpoint).GetCustomAttributes(false).FirstOrDefault();
        Console.WriteLine(route?.GetType().Name);
        class RouteHintAttribute : Attribute { public RouteHintAttribute(string path) => Path = path; public string Path { get; } }
    }
}

    Q8.64 When should you use framework attribute patterns in advanced C#?

    Answer: Framework attribute patterns means routing serialization validation and tooling often rely on attributes to express intent. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with framework magic assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring how metadata is consumed. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_64
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [Obsolete("Use NewFlow")]
        class OldFlow { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(OldFlow).GetCustomAttributes(false).Length);
    }
}

    Q8.65 What problem does attributes versus business logic in advanced C#?

    Answer: Attributes versus business logic means attributes describe metadata while behavior usually belongs in code paths or services. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with full logic inside attributes, and they should avoid the trap of blurring declaration and execution boundaries. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_65
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [MapTo("invoice_total")]
        class InvoiceRow { public decimal Total { get; set; } }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(InvoiceRow).Name);
        class MapToAttribute : Attribute { public MapToAttribute(string field) => Field = field; public string Field { get; } }
    }
}

        [Obsolete("Use NewFlow")]
        class OldFlow { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(OldFlow).GetCustomAttributes(false).Length);
    }
}

    Q8.89 What problem does attributes versus business logic in advanced C#?

    Answer: Attributes versus business logic means attributes describe metadata while behavior usually belongs in code paths or services. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with full logic inside attributes, and they should avoid the trap of blurring declaration and execution boundaries. Example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during an async latency incident, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_89
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [MapTo("invoice_total")]
        class InvoiceRow { public decimal Total { get; set; } }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(InvoiceRow).Name);
        class MapToAttribute : Attribute { public MapToAttribute(string field) => Field = field; public string Field { get; } }
    }
}

    Q8.90 How would you explain attribute interview framing in advanced C#?

    Answer: Attribute interview framing means strong answers explain both declaration and consumption sides. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with square-bracket syntax only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping runtime reading and design trade-offs. Example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_90
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Attributes are useful only when code reads them");
    }
}

    Q8.91 Why is attribute metadata purpose in advanced C#?

    Answer: Attribute metadata purpose means attributes attach declarative metadata to code elements for later inspection. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with comments and naming conventions only, and they should avoid the trap of using attributes with no consumer. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_91
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [Demo("audit")]
        class AuditJob { }
        var attr = typeof(AuditJob).GetCustomAttributes(typeof(DemoAttribute), false).FirstOrDefault();
        Console.WriteLine(attr is not null);
        class DemoAttribute : Attribute { public DemoAttribute(string name) => Name = name; public string Name { get; } }
    }
}

    Q8.92 How can attribute discovery via reflection in advanced C#?

    Answer: Attribute discovery via reflection means attributes matter when runtime code reads them to drive behavior validation or mapping. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with declaration-only thinking, and they should avoid the trap of adding attributes with no processing pipeline. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so the API shape becomes easier to justify.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_92
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [RequiredField]
        class CustomerDto { public string Name { get; set; } = string.Empty; }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(CustomerDto).GetProperties().Length > 0);
        class RequiredFieldAttribute : Attribute { }
    }
}

    Q8.93 What is attribute design and scope in advanced C#?

    Answer: Attribute design and scope means attribute types should express focused metadata clearly rather than become mini configuration objects. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with overloaded metadata bags, and they should avoid the trap of pushing too much behavior into attributes. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_93
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [RouteHint("orders")]
        class OrdersEndpoint { }
        var route = typeof(OrdersEndpoint).GetCustomAttributes(false).FirstOrDefault();
        Console.WriteLine(route?.GetType().Name);
        class RouteHintAttribute : Attribute { public RouteHintAttribute(string path) => Path = path; public string Path { get; } }
    }
}

    Q8.94 How does framework attribute patterns in advanced C#?

    Answer: Framework attribute patterns means routing serialization validation and tooling often rely on attributes to express intent. Teams should focus on it when explaining attributes in real systems, they compare it with framework magic assumptions, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring how metadata is consumed. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo8_94
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        [Obsolete("Use NewFlow")]
        class OldFlow { }
        Console.WriteLine(typeof(OldFlow).GetCustomAttributes(false).Length);
    }
}

    9. Memory and performance tuning

    This section contains 100 interview questions focused on Memory and performance tuning. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

    Q9.1 What is allocation hotspot awareness in advanced C#?

    Answer: Allocation hotspot awareness means performance tuning often starts by finding allocation-heavy paths and reducing churn. Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with CPU-only reasoning, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring allocation cost under load. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo9_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        for (int i = 0; i < 1000; i++)
        {
            var dto = new { Id = i };
        }
        Console.WriteLine("allocations created");
    }
}

    Q9.2 How does boxing closures and hidden allocations in advanced C#?

    Answer: Boxing closures and hidden allocations means advanced C# can allocate in subtle ways through boxing closures iterators and async state machines. Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with new keyword only allocations, and they should avoid the trap of missing hidden runtime costs. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo9_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        int value = 42;
        object boxed = value;
        Console.WriteLine(boxed);
    }
}

    Q9.3 Why does object lifetime and retention in advanced C#?

    Answer: Object lifetime and retention means how long objects stay alive matters as much as how often they are created. Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with allocation counts only, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring retention and promotion. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo9_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var cache = new Dictionary<int, string>();
        cache[803] = "live";
        Console.WriteLine(cache.Count);
    }
}

    Q9.4 When should you use pooling and reuse trade-offs in advanced C#?

    Answer: Pooling and reuse trade-offs means reusing buffers or objects can help in hot paths when done carefully. Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with allocating fresh for every hot-path operation, and they should avoid the trap of pooling everything without measuring complexity costs. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo9_4
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var buffers = new List<byte[]>();
        for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) buffers.Add(new byte[2048]);
        Console.WriteLine(buffers.Sum(x => x.Length));
    }
}

    Q9.5 What problem does profiling before optimizing in advanced C#?

    Answer: Profiling before optimizing means measurement should guide tuning decisions rather than intuition alone. Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with guess-first tuning, and they should avoid the trap of changing code without evidence. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo9_5
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var before = GC.GetTotalMemory(false);
        var list = Enumerable.Range(1, 100).ToList();
        var after = GC.GetTotalMemory(false);
        Console.WriteLine(after >= before);
    }
}

    Q9.6 How would you explain performance interview framing in advanced C#?

    Answer: Performance interview framing means good answers translate tuning into latency throughput and memory behavior. Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with micro-optimization slogans only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping measurement and trade-offs. Example: during a memory pressure review, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo9_6
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Measure before tuning");
    }
}

Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with allocating fresh for every hot-path operation, and they should avoid the trap of pooling everything without measuring complexity costs.     Q9.18 How does performance interview framing in advanced C#?

    Answer: Performance interview framing means good answers translate tuning into latency throughput and memory behavior. Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with micro-optimization slogans only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping measurement and trade-offs. Example: during an async latency incident, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while profiling a LINQ-heavy service, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo9_18
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Measure before tuning");
    }
}

    Q9.19 Why does allocation hotspot awareness in advanced C#?

    Answer: Allocation hotspot awareness means performance tuning often starts by finding allocation-heavy paths and reducing churn. Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with CPU-only reasoning, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring allocation cost under load. Example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during an async latency incident, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo9_19
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        for (int i = 0; i < 1000; i++)
        {
            var dto = new { Id = i };
        }
        Console.WriteLine("allocations created");
    }
}

    Q9.20 When should you use boxing closures and hidden allocations in advanced C#?

    Answer: Boxing closures and hidden allocations means advanced C# can allocate in subtle ways through boxing closures iterators and async state machines. Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with new keyword only allocations, and they should avoid the trap of missing hidden runtime costs. Example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo9_20
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        int value = 42;
        object boxed = value;
        Console.WriteLine(boxed);
    }
}

    Q9.21 What problem does object lifetime and retention in advanced C#?

    Answer: Object lifetime and retention means how long objects stay alive matters as much as how often they are created. Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with allocation counts only, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring retention and promotion. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo9_21
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var cache = new Dictionary<int, string>();
        cache[821] = "live";
        Console.WriteLine(cache.Count);
    }
}

    Q9.22 How would you explain pooling and reuse trade-offs in advanced C#?

    Answer: Pooling and reuse trade-offs means reusing buffers or objects can help in hot paths when done carefully. Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with allocating fresh for every hot-path operation, and they should avoid the trap of pooling everything without measuring complexity costs. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo9_22
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var buffers = new List<byte[]>();
        for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) buffers.Add(new byte[2048]);
        Console.WriteLine(buffers.Sum(x => x.Length));
    }
}

    Q9.23 Why is profiling before optimizing in advanced C#?

    Answer: Profiling before optimizing means measurement should guide tuning decisions rather than intuition alone. Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with guess-first tuning, and they should avoid the trap of changing code without evidence. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo9_23
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var before = GC.GetTotalMemory(false);
        var list = Enumerable.Range(1, 100).ToList();
        var after = GC.GetTotalMemory(false);
        Console.WriteLine(after >= before);
    }
}

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo9_48
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Measure before tuning");
    }
}

    Q9.49 What is allocation hotspot awareness in advanced C#?

    Answer: Allocation hotspot awareness means performance tuning often starts by finding allocation-heavy paths and reducing churn. Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with CPU-only reasoning, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring allocation cost under load. Example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during an async latency incident, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;

        {
