        }
        Console.WriteLine("allocations created");
    }
}

    Q9.50 How does boxing closures and hidden allocations in advanced C#?

    Answer: Boxing closures and hidden allocations means advanced C# can allocate in subtle ways through boxing closures iterators and async state machines. Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with new keyword only allocations, and they should avoid the trap of missing hidden runtime costs. Example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo9_50
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        int value = 42;
        object boxed = value;
        Console.WriteLine(boxed);
    }
}

    Q9.51 Why does object lifetime and retention in advanced C#?

    Answer: Object lifetime and retention means how long objects stay alive matters as much as how often they are created. Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with allocation counts only, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring retention and promotion. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo9_51
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var cache = new Dictionary<int, string>();
        cache[851] = "live";
        Console.WriteLine(cache.Count);
    }
}

    Q9.52 When should you use pooling and reuse trade-offs in advanced C#?

    Answer: Pooling and reuse trade-offs means reusing buffers or objects can help in hot paths when done carefully. Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with allocating fresh for every hot-path operation, and they should avoid the trap of pooling everything without measuring complexity costs. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo9_52
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var buffers = new List<byte[]>();
        for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) buffers.Add(new byte[2048]);
        Console.WriteLine(buffers.Sum(x => x.Length));
    }
}

    Q9.53 What problem does profiling before optimizing in advanced C#?

    Answer: Profiling before optimizing means measurement should guide tuning decisions rather than intuition alone. Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with guess-first tuning, and they should avoid the trap of changing code without evidence. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo9_53
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var before = GC.GetTotalMemory(false);
        var list = Enumerable.Range(1, 100).ToList();
        var after = GC.GetTotalMemory(false);
        Console.WriteLine(after >= before);
    }
}

    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Measure before tuning");
    }
}

    Q9.79 Why is allocation hotspot awareness in advanced C#?

    Answer: Allocation hotspot awareness means performance tuning often starts by finding allocation-heavy paths and reducing churn. Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with CPU-only reasoning, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring allocation cost under load. Example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during an async latency incident, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo9_79
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        for (int i = 0; i < 1000; i++)
        {
            var dto = new { Id = i };
        }
        Console.WriteLine("allocations created");
    }
}

    Q9.80 How can boxing closures and hidden allocations in advanced C#?

    Answer: Boxing closures and hidden allocations means advanced C# can allocate in subtle ways through boxing closures iterators and async state machines. Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with new keyword only allocations, and they should avoid the trap of missing hidden runtime costs. Example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain. Another example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so the API shape becomes easier to justify.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo9_80
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        int value = 42;
        object boxed = value;
        Console.WriteLine(boxed);
    }
}

    Q9.81 What is object lifetime and retention in advanced C#?

    Answer: Object lifetime and retention means how long objects stay alive matters as much as how often they are created. Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with allocation counts only, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring retention and promotion. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo9_81
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var cache = new Dictionary<int, string>();
        cache[881] = "live";
        Console.WriteLine(cache.Count);
    }
}

    Q9.82 How does pooling and reuse trade-offs in advanced C#?

    Answer: Pooling and reuse trade-offs means reusing buffers or objects can help in hot paths when done carefully. Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with allocating fresh for every hot-path operation, and they should avoid the trap of pooling everything without measuring complexity costs. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo9_82
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var buffers = new List<byte[]>();
        for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) buffers.Add(new byte[2048]);
        Console.WriteLine(buffers.Sum(x => x.Length));
    }
}

    Q9.83 Why does profiling before optimizing in advanced C#?

    Answer: Profiling before optimizing means measurement should guide tuning decisions rather than intuition alone. Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with guess-first tuning, and they should avoid the trap of changing code without evidence. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo9_83
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var before = GC.GetTotalMemory(false);
        var list = Enumerable.Range(1, 100).ToList();
        var after = GC.GetTotalMemory(false);
        Console.WriteLine(after >= before);
    }
}

    Q9.84 When should you use performance interview framing in advanced C#?

    Answer: Performance interview framing means good answers translate tuning into latency throughput and memory behavior. Teams should focus on it when explaining memory and performance tuning in real systems, they compare it with micro-optimization slogans only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping measurement and trade-offs. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

    Code Example:

using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

    {
        Console.WriteLine("Measure before tuning");
    }

    10. Advanced collections and APIs

    This section contains 100 interview questions focused on Advanced collections and APIs. Every answer includes a C# code example, and the scenarios rotate so they do not repeat verbatim.

    Q10.1 What problem does specialized collection choice in advanced C#?

    Answer: Specialized collection choice means advanced collection work means matching APIs to access patterns concurrency and ordering needs. Teams should focus on it when explaining advanced collections and apis in real systems, they compare it with List everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of using familiar collections for mismatched workloads. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo10_1
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var set = new HashSet<int> { 1, 2, 2, 3 };
        Console.WriteLine(set.Count);
    }
}

    Q10.2 How would you explain immutable and concurrent collection trade-offs in advanced C#?

    Answer: Immutable and concurrent collection trade-offs means advanced APIs include immutable and concurrent collections with different safety and cost profiles. Teams should focus on it when explaining advanced collections and apis in real systems, they compare it with single collection family for all problems, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring mutation model differences. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo10_2
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var immutable = ImmutableArray.Create(1, 2, 3);
        Console.WriteLine(immutable.Length);
    }
}

    Q10.3 Why is span memory and low allocation APIs in advanced C#?

    Answer: Span memory and low allocation apis means modern APIs like Span and Memory help reduce copies in performance-sensitive code. Teams should focus on it when explaining advanced collections and apis in real systems, they compare it with copy-heavy defaults only, and they should avoid the trap of using low-level APIs without lifetime awareness. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo10_3
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        int[] values = { 1, 2, 3, 4 };
        Span<int> span = values.AsSpan(1, 2);
        Console.WriteLine(span[0]);
    }
}

    Q10.4 How can API shape and capability exposure in advanced C#?

    Answer: Api shape and capability exposure means collection-facing APIs should expose only the capabilities callers actually need. Teams should focus on it when explaining advanced collections and apis in real systems, they compare it with returning concrete mutable types by default, and they should avoid the trap of leaking implementation details. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so the API shape becomes easier to justify.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo10_4
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        IReadOnlyList<int> data = new List<int> { 1, 2, 3 };
        Console.WriteLine(data[0]);
    }
}

    Q10.5 What is advanced BCL usage judgment in advanced C#?

    Answer: Advanced bcl usage judgment means advanced API knowledge matters when it improves clarity safety or performance meaningfully. Teams should focus on it when explaining advanced collections and apis in real systems, they compare it with novelty-driven API use, and they should avoid the trap of using obscure APIs without payoff. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo10_5
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var queue = new ConcurrentQueue<string>();
        queue.Enqueue("a");
        Console.WriteLine(queue.TryDequeue(out var item) ? item : "none");
    }
}

    Q10.6 How does advanced API interview framing in advanced C#?

    Answer: Advanced api interview framing means strong answers connect BCL choices to workload design and maintainability. Teams should focus on it when explaining advanced collections and apis in real systems, they compare it with name-dropping APIs only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping why the choice matters. Example: during a memory pressure review, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

{
    public static void Run()
    {
        Console.WriteLine("Choose advanced APIs only when they improve clarity or performance");
    }

    Q10.9 What problem does span memory and low allocation APIs in advanced C#?

    Answer: Span memory and low allocation apis means modern APIs like Span and Memory help reduce copies in performance-sensitive code. Teams should focus on it when explaining advanced collections and apis in real systems, they compare it with copy-heavy defaults only, and they should avoid the trap of using low-level APIs without lifetime awareness. Example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during an async latency incident, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo10_9
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        int[] values = { 1, 2, 3, 4 };
        Span<int> span = values.AsSpan(1, 2);
        Console.WriteLine(span[0]);
    }
}

    Q10.10 How would you explain API shape and capability exposure in advanced C#?

    Answer: Api shape and capability exposure means collection-facing APIs should expose only the capabilities callers actually need. Teams should focus on it when explaining advanced collections and apis in real systems, they compare it with returning concrete mutable types by default, and they should avoid the trap of leaking implementation details. Example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo10_10
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        IReadOnlyList<int> data = new List<int> { 1, 2, 3 };
        Console.WriteLine(data[0]);
    }
}

    Q10.11 Why is advanced BCL usage judgment in advanced C#?

    Answer: Advanced bcl usage judgment means advanced API knowledge matters when it improves clarity safety or performance meaningfully. Teams should focus on it when explaining advanced collections and apis in real systems, they compare it with novelty-driven API use, and they should avoid the trap of using obscure APIs without payoff. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo10_11
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var queue = new ConcurrentQueue<string>();
        queue.Enqueue("a");
        Console.WriteLine(queue.TryDequeue(out var item) ? item : "none");
    }
}

using System.Collections.Generic;

    Q10.13 What is specialized collection choice in advanced C#?

    Answer: Specialized collection choice means advanced collection work means matching APIs to access patterns concurrency and ordering needs. Teams should focus on it when explaining advanced collections and apis in real systems, they compare it with List everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of using familiar collections for mismatched workloads. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so the API shape becomes easier to justify. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo10_13
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var set = new HashSet<int> { 1, 2, 2, 3 };
        Console.WriteLine(set.Count);
    }
}

    Q10.14 How does immutable and concurrent collection trade-offs in advanced C#?

    Answer: Immutable and concurrent collection trade-offs means advanced APIs include immutable and concurrent collections with different safety and cost profiles. Teams should focus on it when explaining advanced collections and apis in real systems, they compare it with single collection family for all problems, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring mutation model differences. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo10_14
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var immutable = ImmutableArray.Create(1, 2, 3);
        Console.WriteLine(immutable.Length);
    }
}

Example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during an async latency incident, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo10_39
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        int[] values = { 1, 2, 3, 4 };
        Span<int> span = values.AsSpan(1, 2);
        Console.WriteLine(span[0]);
    }
}

    Answer: Api shape and capability exposure means collection-facing APIs should expose only the capabilities callers actually need. Teams should focus on it when explaining advanced collections and apis in real systems, they compare it with returning concrete mutable types by default, and they should avoid the trap of leaking implementation details. Example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;

    Q10.41 What problem does advanced BCL usage judgment in advanced C#?

    Answer: Advanced bcl usage judgment means advanced API knowledge matters when it improves clarity safety or performance meaningfully. Teams should focus on it when explaining advanced collections and apis in real systems, they compare it with novelty-driven API use, and they should avoid the trap of using obscure APIs without payoff. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo10_41
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var queue = new ConcurrentQueue<string>();
        queue.Enqueue("a");
        Console.WriteLine(queue.TryDequeue(out var item) ? item : "none");
    }
}

    Q10.43 Why is specialized collection choice in advanced C#?

    Answer: Specialized collection choice means advanced collection work means matching APIs to access patterns concurrency and ordering needs. Teams should focus on it when explaining advanced collections and apis in real systems, they compare it with List everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of using familiar collections for mismatched workloads. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

{

        Console.WriteLine(set.Count);
    }
}

    Q10.44 How can immutable and concurrent collection trade-offs in advanced C#?

    Answer: Immutable and concurrent collection trade-offs means advanced APIs include immutable and concurrent collections with different safety and cost profiles. Teams should focus on it when explaining advanced collections and apis in real systems, they compare it with single collection family for all problems, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring mutation model differences. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so the API shape becomes easier to justify.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo10_44
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var immutable = ImmutableArray.Create(1, 2, 3);
        Console.WriteLine(immutable.Length);
    }
}

using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo10_69
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        int[] values = { 1, 2, 3, 4 };
        Span<int> span = values.AsSpan(1, 2);
        Console.WriteLine(span[0]);
    }
}

    Q10.70 How does API shape and capability exposure in advanced C#?

    Answer: Api shape and capability exposure means collection-facing APIs should expose only the capabilities callers actually need. Teams should focus on it when explaining advanced collections and apis in real systems, they compare it with returning concrete mutable types by default, and they should avoid the trap of leaking implementation details. Example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the trade-off becomes easier to defend. Another example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo10_70
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        IReadOnlyList<int> data = new List<int> { 1, 2, 3 };
        Console.WriteLine(data[0]);
    }
}

    Q10.71 Why does advanced BCL usage judgment in advanced C#?

    Answer: Advanced bcl usage judgment means advanced API knowledge matters when it improves clarity safety or performance meaningfully. Teams should focus on it when explaining advanced collections and apis in real systems, they compare it with novelty-driven API use, and they should avoid the trap of using obscure APIs without payoff. Example: while reviewing a generic library API, so framework behavior becomes easier to reason about. Another example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the bug becomes easier to isolate.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo10_71
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var queue = new ConcurrentQueue<string>();
        queue.Enqueue("a");
        Console.WriteLine(queue.TryDequeue(out var item) ? item : "none");
    }
}

    Answer: Advanced api interview framing means strong answers connect BCL choices to workload design and maintainability. Teams should focus on it when explaining advanced collections and apis in real systems, they compare it with name-dropping APIs only, and they should avoid the trap of skipping why the choice matters. Example: during a reflection startup audit, so the bug becomes easier to isolate. Another example: while reviewing a generic library API, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict.

using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;

    Q10.73 What problem does specialized collection choice in advanced C#?

    Answer: Specialized collection choice means advanced collection work means matching APIs to access patterns concurrency and ordering needs. Teams should focus on it when explaining advanced collections and apis in real systems, they compare it with List everywhere, and they should avoid the trap of using familiar collections for mismatched workloads. Example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so runtime behavior becomes easier to predict. Another example: during a reflection startup audit, so maintenance risk stays lower.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo10_73
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var set = new HashSet<int> { 1, 2, 2, 3 };
        Console.WriteLine(set.Count);
    }
}

    Q10.74 How would you explain immutable and concurrent collection trade-offs in advanced C#?

    Answer: Immutable and concurrent collection trade-offs means advanced APIs include immutable and concurrent collections with different safety and cost profiles. Teams should focus on it when explaining advanced collections and apis in real systems, they compare it with single collection family for all problems, and they should avoid the trap of ignoring mutation model differences. Example: during a callback bug investigation, so maintenance risk stays lower. Another example: while tuning a collection-heavy data pipeline, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo10_74
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        var immutable = ImmutableArray.Create(1, 2, 3);
        Console.WriteLine(immutable.Length);
    }
}

    Q10.75 Why is span memory and low allocation APIs in advanced C#?

    Answer: Span memory and low allocation apis means modern APIs like Span and Memory help reduce copies in performance-sensitive code. Teams should focus on it when explaining advanced collections and apis in real systems, they compare it with copy-heavy defaults only, and they should avoid the trap of using low-level APIs without lifetime awareness. Example: while stabilizing a worker-thread workflow, so allocation cost becomes easier to spot. Another example: during a callback bug investigation, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo10_75
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        int[] values = { 1, 2, 3, 4 };
        Span<int> span = values.AsSpan(1, 2);
        Console.WriteLine(span[0]);
    }
}

        int[] values = { 1, 2, 3, 4 };
        Span<int> span = values.AsSpan(1, 2);
        Console.WriteLine(span[0]);
    }
}

    Q10.100 How can API shape and capability exposure in advanced C#?

    Answer: Api shape and capability exposure means collection-facing APIs should expose only the capabilities callers actually need. Teams should focus on it when explaining advanced collections and apis in real systems, they compare it with returning concrete mutable types by default, and they should avoid the trap of leaking implementation details. Example: during a plugin-style extension redesign, so the abstraction boundary becomes easier to explain. Another example: while validating attribute-driven behavior, so the API shape becomes easier to justify.

    Code Example:

    using System;
using System.Collections.Concurrent;
using System.Collections.Generic;
using System.Collections.Immutable;
using System.Linq;
using System.Reflection;
using System.Threading;
using System.Threading.Tasks;

public static class Demo10_100
{
    public static void Run()
    {
        IReadOnlyList<int> data = new List<int> { 1, 2, 3 };
        Console.WriteLine(data[0]);
    }
}
    Made with Material for MkDocs
